data_source: Berkshire Hathaway 2014 special letter 'Berkshire – Past, Present and Future' and Berkshire 1972/1976 shareholder letters

g4_revenue_trend.description: The New England textile industry had been in long-term decline for decades. Berkshire's own 1954 board minutes said the trend must continue until supply and demand balanced.

g5_gross_margin_trend.description: By Buffett's later description, return on sales and return on capital employed were inadequate and the company had already suffered years of operating losses before control changed hands.

g7_moat.description: Berkshire textile had no durable competitive advantage. It was a capital-intensive commodity manufacturer in a region losing cost competitiveness, unable to change the industry's direction even though the problems were long understood.

control_groups.conclusion: PASS — same structurally declining textile economics with no moat.
PASS — a larger operator, but still trapped in a deteriorating commodity textile industry.

buffett_action: Buffett Partnership took control of Berkshire Hathaway in 1965 after accumulating 392,633 shares.
actual_result: Buffett later called the decision monumentally stupid and eventually shut the textile operation in 1985 after two decades of frustration.
key_insight: Cheap working capital and book value can still be a trap when the business is structurally dying and every dollar stays imprisoned in the operation.
